title: Expanding Windows: The Growing History
concept: Expanding windows calculate statistics over the entire past history up to the current step, creating a growing memory of all cumulative data.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why might a restaurant model need an 'Is_Holiday' calendar feature?

options.text: Because holiday closures or special events drastically change customer behavior compared to a normal day.
Because holidays are always on the first day of every month.
Because models are required by law to rest on holidays.

exercises.explain: Correct! Calendar features like holiday flags tell the model to expect departures from normal weekly or daily routines.

cards.title: The lifetime scoreboard
cards.body: Imagine you are playing a video game. To see how you're doing right now, you might look at your score in the current level (like a rolling window). But to see your overall progress since you first started playing, you look at your lifetime high score. In forecasting, sometimes a model needs a lifetime scoreboard of all data up to the present moment.

cards.title: What is an Expanding Window?
cards.body: An expanding window starts at the beginning of your time series and grows larger with every new step. Unlike a rolling window that discards old data to maintain a fixed size, an expanding window keeps all past history, calculating statistics (like the running mean or max) of everything seen so far.

cards.title: A snowball rolling down a hill
cards.body: Think of a rolling window like a bicycle wheel: it stays the same size as it moves. Think of an expanding window like a rolling snowball: as it rolls forward, it collects more and more snow, growing larger and larger, carrying the memory of every path it has traveled.

cards.title: Lifetime maximum sales
cards.body: An online store wants to know if a current sales spike is unprecedented. It creates an expanding window feature: Cumulative_Max_Sales. On Day 100, this feature is the highest single-day sales value recorded from Day 1 to Day 99. The model uses this to judge if today's demand is truly extraordinary.

cards.title: Not always better than rolling
cards.body: You might assume that more history is always better. However, expanding windows can dilute recent trends. If a business grew rapidly 5 years ago but has stabilized recently, the expanding average will still be influenced by those old, low numbers. For recent trends, rolling windows are often better.

cards.title: Rolling vs. Expanding Windows
cards.body: Comparing how the windows cover history at Day 5:

left.text:
Covers a fixed slice:
Includes Day 2, Day 3, and Day 4.
(Day 1 is forgotten!)

right.text:
Covers all history:
Includes Day 1, Day 2, Day 3, and Day 4.
(Zero data is forgotten!)

exercises.prompt: How does the size of an expanding window change as you move forward in time?

options.text: It grows larger to include all past steps.
It stays exactly the same size.
It shrinks to focus only on the most recent step.
It resets to one step every Monday.

exercises.explain: Correct! An expanding window grows with each step, incorporating the entire history up to the current point.

exercises.prompt: True or false: An expanding average is highly sensitive to sudden, temporary changes that happened yesterday.
exercises.explain: False! Because an expanding average includes the entire history, a single yesterday spike is diluted by all the other past days.

exercises.sentence: To track the all-time highest temperature recorded since a weather station opened, you should use an ___ window.
exercises.explain: An expanding window is perfect for tracking running totals, cumulative counts, or all-time maximums.

exercises.prompt: Which of these represents an expanding window feature?

options.text: The running total of all items sold since the store first opened.
The total sales from the last 7 days.
A flag indicating if it is raining today.

exercises.explain: Correct! A running total since the beginning represents an expanding window calculation.

exercises.prompt: When would you prefer a rolling window average over an expanding window average?

options.text: When recent patterns are much more relevant to the future than ancient history.
When you want to save the maximum amount of history.
When you are predicting calendar holidays.
When the time series has no noise.

exercises.explain: Exactly! If your data's patterns change over time, old history can mislead your model. A rolling average focuses only on the recent past, whereas an expanding average gets dragged down by old historical levels. Next: the biggest trap in time series feature engineering!
